title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis

patient.name: Ahmed Hassan
patient.persona: university student, type 1 diabetes, non-compliant with insulin

chief_complaint: I've been vomiting all day. My stomach hurts. I feel so weak and thirsty. I can't catch my breath.

physical_exam.General: Thin young male, Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing), fruity odor on breath, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor
physical_exam.Abdomen: Diffuse tenderness, no guarding or rebound, hypoactive bowel sounds

hidden_data.abg: pH 7.12, PaCO2 22 mmHg, PaO2 98 mmHg, HCO3 8 mmol/L, SaO2 98%
hidden_data.urinalysis: Glucose 4+, Ketones 3+, No leukocytes or nitrites
hidden_data.cmp: Sodium 128 (corrected Na: 134 - pseudohyponatremia from hyperglycemia), Potassium 5.8, Chloride 98, HCO3 8, BUN 32, Creatinine 1.4, Glucose 485
hidden_data.ecg: Sinus tachycardia. Peaked T waves (hyperkalemia). No ST changes.

key_learning_points: DKA triad: Hyperglycemia (glucose >250 mg/dL) + Ketosis (beta-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L or urine ketones ≥2+) + Metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, HCO3 <15)
Anion gap metabolic acidosis = Na - (Cl + HCO3) >12. DKA produces high anion gap from ketoacids (acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate)
Kussmaul respirations = compensatory respiratory alkalosis for metabolic acidosis. Deep, rapid breathing to blow off CO2
Potassium is always depleted (3-5 mEq/kg deficit) despite initial serum K+ often being elevated (insulin deficiency → K+ shifts extracellularly)
Treatment: IV fluids (0.9% NaCl), IV insulin infusion, potassium replacement, and identify/treat precipitating cause

patient_responses.onset: I ran out of my insulin pump supplies 3 days ago. I thought I could manage without until my appointment next week. I started feeling sick yesterday.
patient_responses.symptoms: I've been vomiting since this morning. Can't keep anything down. My mouth is so dry. I'm peeing constantly. I feel like I can't breathe normally.
patient_responses.diabetes: I've been type 1 diabetic since I was 12. I use an insulin pump with Novolog. My last HbA1c was 9.5%. I admit I don't check my sugars as often as I should.
patient_responses.precipitant: I've been stressed with finals. I might have a cold - my roommate was sick. I haven't been eating well, just pizza and energy drinks.

hints.history: Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, vomiting, abdominal pain in known diabetic. Ask about insulin non-compliance, infection symptoms, and recent stressors.
hints.examination: Look for Kussmaul respirations (pathognomonic for severe acidosis), fruity breath odor (acetone), signs of dehydration (tachycardia, hypotension, dry membranes).
hints.labs: Glucose, ABG (pH, HCO3, PaCO2), serum ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate), electrolytes (anion gap, K+, Na+), BUN/Cr, serum osmolality, HbA1c.
hints.imaging: CXR to evaluate for pneumonia. ECG for hyperkalemia. CT abdomen if pancreatitis suspected (lipase elevated).

feedback.correct_message: Excellent! You correctly diagnosed DKA based on hyperglycemia (485 mg/dL), anion gap metabolic acidosis (pH 7.12, HCO3 8, AG 22), and elevated beta-hydroxybutyrate (5.8 mmol/L). The Kussmaul respirations were the key clinical clue. Management requires IV fluids, insulin infusion, and careful potassium monitoring.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good job recognizing hyperglycemia! However, the ABG shows severe anion gap metabolic acidosis with elevated ketones - this is DKA, not just hyperglycemia or HHS. HHS presents with hyperglycemia and hyperosmolality WITHOUT significant ketosis or acidosis. DKA requires IV insulin, not just fluids.
feedback.incorrect_message: This is classic DKA: Hyperglycemia (485) + anion gap metabolic acidosis (pH 7.12, HCO3 8, AG 22) + ketosis (BHB 5.8) + Kussmaul respirations. The patient needs immediate IV fluids (0.9% NaCl 1L/hr initially), IV regular insulin (0.1 U/kg/hr), and potassium replacement once K+ <5.3. Admit to ICU for hourly glucose monitoring.
clinical_pearls: Correction formula for Na in hyperglycemia: For every 100 mg/dL glucose above 100, add 2.4 mEq/L to measured Na
Potassium falls rapidly with insulin therapy (insulin drives K+ into cells). Start K+ replacement when serum K+ <5.3 mEq/L
Transition to subcutaneous insulin ONLY when anion gap closed, HCO3 >15, pH >7.3, and patient tolerating PO

glucose.comment: Hyperglycemia 485 confirms diabetes as cause. Must check hourly during DKA management

abg.comment: pH 7.12, HCO3 8, PaCO2 22 = severe anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation (Winter formula: expected PaCO2 = 1.5(HCO3)+8±2 = 18-22)

serum_ketones.comment: Beta-hydroxybutyrate 5.8 confirms ketosis. Will trend to monitor DKA resolution

cmp.comment: Anion gap 22, K+ 5.8 (will drop with insulin), Cr 1.4 (pre-renal from dehydration), corrected Na 134

ecg.comment: Peaked T waves from hyperkalemia. Monitor for changes during therapy

missing_tests: Blood cultures if febrile, Urine culture if UTI symptoms, Lipase if abdominal pain severe - to identify precipitating infection
feedback.attending_note: Doctor, DKA protocol: 1) IV 0.9% NaCl 1L over first hour, then 500mL/hr ×4h, then 250mL/hr. 2) IV Regular insulin 0.1 U/kg bolus then 0.1 U/kg/hr infusion. 3) When glucose <250, add D5½NS. 4) K+ replacement when K+ <5.3. 5) Hourly glucose, q2h electrolytes initially. Admit to ICU. Identify precipitant (infection, non-compliance). Diabetes education before discharge.
